text: The surgeon has fully mobilized the right colon and transected the mesentery at the planned sites, preparing for the next steps in the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon uses the Firefly imaging system to assess perfusion at the planned anastomosis site. An anti-mesenteric suture is placed on both the colon and the small bowel to facilitate the creation of the anastomosis.

The surgeon uses a twelve millimeter right lower quadrant port for staple placement during the procedure. A suture is cut to twenty centimeters, allowing it to be used twice for stay sutures, with one placed proximally and another at eight centimeters distally.
The surgeon uses the additional robotic arm to hold the small bowel in place, while a short suture is applied for orientation purposes during the procedure.

The surgeon navigates underneath the mesentery, revealing a wide-open defect for stapler passage. The colon is opened slightly medial toward the mesenteric side to stabilize it for stapling. The surgeon removes a portion of the remaining ligament of Treitz and opens the small bowel on the anti-mesenteric side, using suction as needed.

The surgeon positions the stapler with its thin side towards the small bowel and the large side towards the colon, ensuring proper alignment for a wide anastomosis. The stay suture is held to allow the mesentery to fall away, facilitating full insertion of the stapler. The stapler is lifted anteriorly to the abdominal wall to ensure complete mesentery clearance before closing and firing it.

The surgeon reaches in to grasp the back wall of the common enterotomy collotomy, ensuring the second stapler fully removes all specimen side tissue. This action is performed distal to the proximal stay suture, with visible perfusion up to the planned transection site and lack of perfusion on the specimen side.

The surgeon uses the distal stay suture to orient the specimen, ensuring a perpendicular staple across the original staple line. The mesenteria is fully mobilized to prevent bleeding during the second staple firing, which is performed in a perpendicular manner.
The surgeon places the specimen into a large thoracic endo catch bag through a fifteen millimeter port at the extraction site.
